1.synthesis: Isaiah 23 is the final and culminating burden oracle of chapters 13–23: the burden of Tyre. Tyre was the mercantile heart of the ancient Mediterranean world, the Phoenician city whose ships ranged from the Lebanon coast to the far west, establishing colonies and controlling trade networks. The oracle begins with the ships of Tarshish receiving word of Tyre’s destruction: “Wail, O ships of Tarshish, for Tyre is laid waste, without house or harbor! From the land of Cyprus it is revealed to them” (23:1). The merchant city whose traders were princes, whose merchants were the honored of the earth (23:8), is destroyed. Who has purposed this against Tyre? “The LORD of hosts has purposed it, to defile the pompous pride of all glory, to dishonor all the honored of the earth” (23:9). The theological diagnosis is the same as for Babylon: the pride of commercial and political achievement that has forgotten the source of all prosperity. The Chaldeans are named as the instrument of destruction: they will make Tyre a ruin, raise up the siege towers, strip the palaces (23:13). The ships of Tarshish are told to wail, for their fortress is destroyed (23:14).
1.historical_context: Tyre was besieged by Nebuchadnezzar for 13 years (585–572 BC) and later conquered by Alexander the Great in 332 BC after building a causeway to the island city. The oracle against Tyre is one of the most frequently interpreted prophetic texts in terms of historical fulfillment, with Ezekiel 26–28 providing the most extended parallel. The merchants of Tyre were the economic engine of the ancient world; their purple dye (from the murex shellfish) gave the color of royalty its name (Phoenicia = land of purple). The dishonoring of “all the honored of the earth” (v. 9) makes Tyre’s fall not merely political but a theological statement about the limits of commercial glory.
1.literary_structure: The unit moves from the wailing of the ships (v. 1) through the mourning of the coastlands (vv. 2–7) through the theological indictment (vv. 8–9) through the divine counsel against Tyre (vv. 10–12) through the identification of the Chaldeans as instrument (v. 13) through the closing wail (v. 14). The repetition of the wailing ships at beginning and end creates an inclusio of grief around the theological explanation at the center.
1.theological_application: The “pompous pride of all glory” that YHWH purposes to defile (23:9) applies the same theological principle to commercial achievement that the Babylon oracle applied to military and political power: every form of human glory that becomes its own end, that forgets the source of all prosperity, is under divine judgment. Tyre represents the economic dimension of human self-exaltation; its fall is as theologically necessary as Babylon’s. Communities shaped by this oracle are warned against the idolatry of commercial success.
1.christology: The merchants of Tyre who were the honored of the earth (23:8) are the Old Testament type of the merchants of Babylon whose wealth is mourned in Revelation 18: “the merchants of the earth weep and mourn for her, since no one buys their cargo anymore” (Rev 18:11). The YHWH who purposed the defilement of commercial pride is the same God who in Christ declared: “You cannot serve God and money” (Matt 6:24). The commercial empire, however impressive, cannot survive the divine verdict against its pride.

15.synthesis: The oracle against Tyre closes with a unique eschatological reversal: Tyre will be forgotten for seventy years, like the days of one king, and at the end of seventy years Tyre will sing the song of the harlot (23:15). The harlot metaphor is striking: Tyre will return to her trade like a forgotten harlot returning to the streets to gain clients. She will go around the city, playing the lyre and singing many songs: “Take a harp, go about the city, O forgotten prostitute! Make sweet melody; sing many songs, that you may be remembered” (23:16). The result: at the end of the seventy years, the LORD will visit Tyre, and she will return to her wages and prostitute herself with all the kingdoms of the world on the face of the earth (23:17). But then the stunning transformation: “Her gain and her wages will be holy to the LORD. It will not be stored or hoarded, but her gain will supply abundant food and fine clothing for those who dwell before the LORD” (23:18). The commercial wealth of Tyre will be consecrated to YHWH and used for the sustenance of his people.
15.historical_context: The seventy-year period of Tyre’s forgetting (v. 15) parallels the seventy years of Babylon’s dominance in Jeremiah 25:11–12; the number may be conventional for a generation or a full period. The harlot metaphor for Tyre’s commercial activity is the same used in Ezekiel 27–28: the prostitution is not sexual but commercial, the promiscuous engagement with all nations in trade for gain. The eschatological reversal of v. 18—Tyre’s gain becoming holy to YHWH—is a remarkable application of the consecration vocabulary (qodesh, holy) to commercial profit: even the wealth of the greatest trading city will be incorporated into the service of YHWH’s purposes.
15.literary_structure: The unit moves from the period of forgetting (vv. 15–16) through the return to trade (v. 17) through the eschatological consecration (v. 18). The movement from harlot to holy is the structural climax: the very commercial activity that was judged (prostitution to all kingdoms) becomes consecrated when its proceeds are directed to those who dwell before YHWH. The transformation is not of the activity but of its orientation and destination.
15.theological_application: The consecration of Tyre’s commercial gain to YHWH (23:18) is the eschatological vision in which even the wealth of the nations—wealth acquired through means not characterized by covenant faithfulness—is incorporated into the service of God’s kingdom. This is the completion of the Isaiah 19:23–25 vision: not just Egypt and Assyria in the community of worship but Tyre’s commercial wealth consecrated to YHWH. The ultimate destination of all wealth is the sustenance of those who dwell before the LORD.
15.christology: The consecration of Tyre’s gain to YHWH’s purposes (23:18) is the Old Testament anticipation of the nations bringing their glory into the New Jerusalem: “and the kings of the earth will bring their glory into it...they will bring into it the glory and the honor of the nations” (Rev 21:24–26). In Christ, who declared that the wealth of the sinner is laid up for the righteous (Prov 13:22), the commercial gain of Tyre finds its eschatological orientation: all things are made new and consecrated to the service of the King and his people.
